Kala Sound Engine Overview

Introduction

The Kala sound engine is a modular synthesis system that supports three distinct approaches to sound generation — spectral synthesis, physical modeling, and wavetable synthesis — all within the same visual graph framework. Rather than fixing a single synthesis method, Kala lets composers combine techniques freely: route a physical model's output through spectral shapers, layer a wavetable texture behind a harmonic generator, or use any combination the instrument demands.

Sound synthesis in Kala is treated as composition: a deliberate, expressive act rather than a selection from presets. The same modular graph that defines the timbre also defines how that timbre evolves over the course of a note — through envelopes, physics-based smoothing, oscillators, and drift.

Crucially, the expressive gesture you put into a note on the Score Canvas — pen pressure, a drawn dynamics curve — is not only applied to volume. It is a first-class signal inside the sound engine. An Envelope Engine with its Follow Dynamics mode active re-exposes that dynamics curve as a control signal that can drive any parameter in the graph: timbre, brightness, breathiness, filter cutoff, vibrato depth. The phrasing you compose is the modulation; no separate automation lane is needed.

What is a Sounit?

A Sounit (sound unit) is the fundamental building block of Kala's sound generation system. Unlike traditional synthesizers where a "patch" is simply a collection of parameter values, a Sounit is a complete, self-contained definition of how sound is created.

Key Characteristics

  • Visual Graph: A Sounit is represented as a directed graph on the SounitBuilder canvas, showing containers (nodes) and their connections (edges)
  • Reusable Blueprint: Sounits can be saved, loaded, and shared between projects
  • Per-Track Assignment: Each track in a composition can have its own Sounit, allowing different timbres to coexist
  • Real-Time Evaluation: The Sounit graph is executed sample-by-sample during audio rendering

Philosophy

The Sounit concept embodies Kala's core philosophy: sound synthesis should be as expressive as composition itself. Rather than selecting from preset sounds, composers build their own instruments from first principles — and those instruments live and breathe through the notes they play.

The Container System

Containers are the visual and functional units that make up a Sounit graph. Each container represents a specific audio processing operation.

Container Architecture

Every container has:

  • Type: What kind of processing it performs (e.g., Harmonic Generator, Karplus Strong)
  • Instance Name: User-editable identifier, unique within a Sounit
  • Color: Visual category indicator (blue / orange / green / purple)
  • Ports: Input and output connection points
  • Parameters: Configurable values that control behavior, adjustable from the inspector

Container Categories

Essential (Blue) — Sound Generators

Blue containers produce sound. A valid Sounit graph must include at least one blue generator and must end with a Spectrum to Signal or produce a direct signal output.

Container Synthesis Approach Key Inputs Output
Harmonic Generator Spectral — builds a harmonic series from DNA presets purity, drift spectrum
Spectrum to Signal Spectral — converts a harmonic spectrum to an audio waveform spectrumIn, pitch, normalize signalOut
Karplus Strong Physical modeling — plucked and bowed string simulation signalIn, trigger, damping, pluckPosition signalOut
Recorder Physical modeling — digital waveguide fipple flute breathPressure, jetRatio, noiseGain signalOut
Bowed Physical modeling — digital waveguide bowed string bowPressure, bowVelocity, bowPosition signalOut
Reed Physical modeling — digital waveguide conical-bore reed instrument breathPressure, reedStiffness, blowPosition signalOut
Attack Physical modeling — STK-inspired instrument onset transients (flute chiff, brass buzz, bow scratch, piano hammer, etc.) signalIn, trigger, attackType, duration, intensity signalOut
Wavetable Synth Wavetable — cycles through a stored waveform table position spectrum, signalOut
Signal Mixer Utility — weighted sum of two audio signals signalA, signalB, gainA, gainB signalOut

Shaping (Orange) — Spectral & Timbral Transformation

Orange containers reshape sound after it is generated. They operate on spectra (before Spectrum to Signal) or on audio signals (after generation).

Container Purpose Key Inputs Output
Rolloff Processor Controls brightness by attenuating high and low harmonics spectrumIn, lowRolloff, highRolloff, crossover spectrumOut
Spectrum Blender Crossfades between two harmonic spectra spectrumA, spectrumB, position spectrumOut
Formant Body Vowel-like resonance using two bandpass filters (vocal tract model) signalIn, f1Freq, f2Freq, f1Q, f2Q, directMix signalOut
Breath Turbulence Blends a harmonic voice signal with noise for breathiness voiceIn, noiseIn, blend signalOut
Noise Color Filter Generates spectrally shaped noise (white, pink, brown, etc.) color, filterQ noiseOut

Modifiers (Green) — Time-Varying Parameter Control

Green containers produce control signals that modulate parameters of other containers. They do not generate audio directly; their outputs connect to input ports of blue or orange containers.

Container Purpose Key Inputs Output
Envelope Engine Evaluates a curve against note progress (0.0–1.0) timeScale, valueScale, valueOffset envelopeValue
Physics System Mass-spring-damper: parameters approach targets with inertia targetValue, mass, springK, damping currentValue
LFO Low-frequency oscillator with selectable waveform frequency, amplitude, waveType valueOut
Drift Engine Slow random walk for subtle organic instability amount, rate driftOut
Easing Applicator Applies a shaped curve between two values over a progress input startValue, endValue, progress, easingSelect easedValue
Frequency Mapper Maps the note's pitch (Hz) to a normalized control value curve controlOut

Filters (Purple) — Signal Processing

Purple containers apply DSP-based processing to audio signals. Unlike orange shapers which operate on harmonic spectra, filters work in the time domain on the final audio signal and can be placed anywhere in the post-generation chain.

Container Purpose Key Inputs Output
10-Band EQ Graphic equalizer with ten frequency bands signalIn, band1–band10 signalOut
Comb Filter Delay-based comb filtering for resonance and metallic textures signalIn, delayTime, feedback, damping signalOut
LP/HP Filter Resonant low-pass / high-pass filter with switchable mode signalIn, cutoff, resonance signalOut
IR Convolution Convolves the signal with a loaded impulse response (room, body, etc.) signalIn, wetDry, predelay, highDamp, lowCut signalOut

Port Data Types

Containers communicate through typed ports:

  • Spectrum: Array of harmonic amplitudes (index = harmonic number)
  • Signal: Audio sample value (typically -1.0 to 1.0)
  • Control: Parameter value (typically 0.0 to 1.0)
  • Trigger: Impulse/gate signal (rising edge detection)

The Connection System

Connections define how data flows between containers in a Sounit graph.

Connection Properties

Each connection has:

  • Source: Output port of the sending container
  • Destination: Input port of the receiving container
  • Function: How the incoming value combines with existing input
  • Weight: Scaling factor for weighted operations (0.0 to 1.0)

Connection Functions

Function Formula Use Case
passthrough output = sourceValue Direct signal or spectrum routing
add output = current + source × weight Additive modulation
multiply output = current × source × weight Amplitude / gain control
subtract output = current − source × weight Inverse control
replace output = current × (1−weight) + source × weight Smooth parameter crossfading
modulate output = current + (source − 0.5) × weight × 2.0 Bipolar CV-style modulation

Signal Flow Rules

  1. Direction: Connections flow from output ports to input ports only
  2. No Cycles: Feedback loops are not allowed (detected during graph compilation)
  3. Multiple Inputs: A single input port can receive multiple connections (combined via their respective functions)
  4. Auto-Direction: The UI automatically corrects direction if you start drawing a connection from an input port

Audio Rendering Pipeline

Overview

Note Input
    |
    v
[Audio Engine — Real-time synthesis via RTAudio]
    |
    v
[SounitGraph — Topological execution order]
    |
    v
[Container Processors — Per-sample generation]
    |
    v
[Signal Output — Speaker]

Step 1: Graph Compilation

When a Sounit is loaded or modified, the SounitGraph compiles it:

  1. Snapshot Connections: Caches all connections at build time
  2. Create Processors: Instantiates processor objects for each container
  3. Topological Sort: Uses Kahn's algorithm to compute execution order
  4. Cycle Detection: Reports errors if feedback loops exist
  5. Identify Output: Locates the final Spectrum to Signal container (or direct signal output)

Step 2: Per-Sample Execution

For each audio sample, the graph executes all containers in topological order:

for each container in execution order:
    1. Query incoming connections
    2. Apply connection functions to combine inputs
    3. Execute container's processor
    4. Store outputs for downstream containers

Step 3: Note Parameters

Each sample receives contextual information from the note being rendered:

  • pitch: Fundamental frequency in Hz
  • noteProgress: Position in note lifecycle (0.0 = start, 1.0 = end)
  • isLegato: Whether to skip the attack phase (for tied notes)
  • dynamics: The note's dynamics curve value at the current position (0.0–1.0) — captured from pen pressure or drawn manually. Used directly by the audio engine for volume, and also available as a control signal via the Envelope Engine's Follow Dynamics mode.

Synthesis Approaches

Kala's engine supports three synthesis paradigms, which can be used independently or combined in a single graph.

Spectral Synthesis

The original Kala approach. The Harmonic Generator builds a spectrum — an array of harmonic amplitudes — using DNA presets: acoustic profiles that model the characteristic harmonic distribution of voices, strings, brass, reeds, and other instruments. The spectrum is then shaped (Rolloff Processor, Spectrum Blender, Formant Body) before Spectrum to Signal converts it to an audio waveform by summing harmonic oscillators at the correct pitch.

The key advantage of working in the spectral domain is independent control over each harmonic. An envelope can brighten the sound over time by modulating a rolloff parameter, or a drift engine can apply per-harmonic micro-detuning to create organic instability — effects that are difficult or impossible to achieve in the time domain.

Physical Modeling

Physical modeling containers simulate the acoustics of real instruments directly, producing audio signals without going through the spectrum pathway. These containers are derived from STK (Synthesis Toolkit) digital waveguide algorithms:

  • Karplus Strong: A delay-line model of a vibrating string. Excite it with a pluck (noise burst) or a sustained signal, and the recirculating delay with low-pass filtering produces the natural decay and tone of a plucked or hammered string.
  • Recorder: A digital waveguide model of a fipple flute. Breath pressure, jet ratio, and noise drive a tube resonator to produce flute-like tones with physically meaningful control over breathiness and embouchure.
  • Bowed: A bowed string model using a slip-stick friction nonlinearity. Bow pressure, velocity, and position on the string produce the characteristic bowed string sound with natural resonances.
  • Reed: A conical-bore reed instrument model (in the style of a saxophone). Controls breath pressure and reed stiffness to produce the characteristic overblown, buzzy quality of reed instruments.
  • Attack: Not a sustained sound generator, but a library of onset transients — the sounds an instrument makes at the moment of articulation. Attack types include flute chiff, brass buzz, bow scratch, piano hammer strike, and drum hit. Place an Attack container in parallel with a sustain generator to give notes realistic beginnings.

Wavetable Synthesis

The Wavetable Synth stores a table of waveform samples and cycles through it at the playback pitch. It can output directly as a signal or export its instantaneous spectrum for use by downstream shaping containers. A position input allows morphing through the wavetable over time — controlled by an envelope or LFO — producing the classic wavetable sweep effect. Custom wavetables can be loaded from WAV files.

Physics-Based Parameter Animation

Regardless of which synthesis approach generates the sound, Kala favors physically plausible parameter movement over abrupt jumps. Several green modifier containers embody this:

  • Physics System: A mass-spring-damper that makes any parameter approach its target with inertia. The same system that describes a door closing slowly, a pendulum swinging, or water settling in a glass. Underdamped settings produce overshoot and oscillation; overdamped settings produce a slow, heavy approach.
  • Drift Engine: A slow random walk layered onto any parameter — pitch, brightness, amplitude — to simulate the natural instability of acoustic instruments. Unlike random noise, drift has memory: it wanders continuously rather than jumping.
  • LFO: A periodic oscillator at sub-audio rates, useful for vibrato, tremolo, and cyclic timbral modulation.
  • Envelope Engine: Evaluates a user-drawn curve against note progress, mapping the lifetime of a note to any shape of parameter evolution. Its Follow Dynamics (~) toggle bypasses the envelope shape entirely and instead routes the note's own dynamics curve as the output — turning the expressive gesture you composed on the Score Canvas into a live control signal for any downstream parameter.

Multi-Track Architecture

Track Independence

  • Each track in a composition can have its own independent Sounit
  • Different tracks can use completely different synthesis approaches — one track might use spectral synthesis for a vocal timbre while another uses Karplus Strong for a plucked string
  • Tracks maintain separate parameter states and processor instances

Variation System

Tracks can have Sounit Variations: multiple synthesis graphs for the same track, enabling timbral exploration without losing previous work. Individual notes in the Score Canvas can be assigned to specific variations, allowing a single instrument track to produce multiple timbres across a phrase.

Creating Variations

There are two ways to create a named variation for a track:

  • From the current canvas: Click the Create Variation button in the Sound Engine toolbar (or choose it from the Sound menu). This takes a snapshot of the graph as it currently stands and stores it as a new named variation. The base Sounit is left untouched.
  • From a saved file: Use Sound > Load Sounit and select a .sounit file. If the target track already has a Sounit loaded, a dialog appears offering three choices:
    • Load as variation (default) — the file is compiled into a new variation without disturbing the base Sounit already on the track. You will be prompted to name the variation; the name stored inside the file is offered as a default.
    • Replace base sounit — the current base Sounit is discarded and replaced by the loaded file, as if loading onto an empty track.
    • Cancel — no change is made.
    Loading saved files as variations is the primary way to build a library of alternative timbres for a single track — save different versions of your graph as .sounit files and reload them later as variations.

Managing Variations

All variations for the current track are listed in the Variations panel (Sound Engine tab, right side). From there you can rename or delete any variation. The base Sounit is always shown at the top of the list and cannot be deleted. Assigning a variation to a specific note is done from the note inspector in the Score Canvas tab.

Note Mode — Per-Note Sound Design

The Problem It Solves

By default, every note in a track is rendered with the same Sounit graph. The Variation System lets you build and name multiple alternative graphs for a track, but assigning a specific variation to a specific note is an indirect process. Note Mode makes it direct.

Select a single note in the Score Canvas, switch to the Sound Engine tab, and enter Note Mode. The canvas immediately shows the graph that belongs to that exact note. Edit freely — add containers, change parameters, rewire connections — and every change applies to that note alone. Navigate to the next note and you are editing its graph. The composition stays intact; you are sculpting sounds note by note.

How to Enter Note Mode

  1. Select exactly one note in the Score Canvas. Note Mode requires a single selection — selecting no notes or more than one note will display a warning and refuse to activate.
  2. Switch to the Sound Engine tab (Tab key toggles between Score Canvas and Sound Engine).
  3. Click the green "Note Mode" button in the toolbar, or press N.

The button turns red to confirm Note Mode is active. The status bar at the bottom of the Sound Engine window shows the current note's position in the phrase, its pitch, and which variation graph it is using.

What You Can Do in Note Mode

  • Edit the graph freely — add, remove, or reconfigure containers and connections. Changes are local to the current note.
  • Navigate between notes — press ← or → to move to the previous or next note on the track. The canvas updates to reflect each note's graph. Your edits to the previous note are saved automatically before moving.
  • Preview the current note — press Space to render and play just the selected note using its current graph. Press Space again to stop.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you enter Note Mode, Kala takes a snapshot of the track's base Sounit graph and holds it in memory. As you edit in Note Mode, each note's changes are stored as an internal variation — a private copy of the graph that is linked to that specific note and saved with the project.

Notes you never touch in Note Mode continue to use the track's base Sounit. Note Mode only overrides notes you explicitly edit. When the composition is rendered, each note is sent through its own variation graph (or the base graph if no variation was assigned).

Exiting Note Mode

Press Esc, or click the "Note Mode" button again. On exit, the canvas returns to the track's base Sounit graph, and all per-note customizations are preserved inside the note data. The Tab key is disabled while Note Mode is active — you must exit first before switching tabs.

Keyboard Shortcuts in Note Mode

Key Action
← / → Navigate to previous / next note (auto-saves current note's graph)
Space Play the current note / stop playback
Esc Exit Note Mode, restore base Sounit graph
Tab Blocked while in Note Mode — exit first

Data Persistence

Sounit File Format (JSON)

Sounits are saved as .sounit JSON files containing the container list (type, position, parameters) and the connection list (from/to container and port, function, weight). Custom data — envelope curves, wavetable samples, impulse responses, DNA patterns — is embedded in the container's JSON object.

Summary

The Kala sound engine has grown into a multi-paradigm synthesis platform built around one organizing idea: the modular graph. Within that graph, composers can combine:

  • Spectral synthesis — harmonic generators and spectrum shapers for instruments built from acoustic DNA
  • Physical modeling — waveguide instruments (Karplus Strong, Recorder, Bowed, Reed) and onset transients (Attack) for physically grounded timbres
  • Wavetable synthesis — sample-based waveform cycling with morph control
  • DSP filters — EQ, comb, LP/HP, and convolution reverb in the post-generation chain
  • Physics-based modulation — envelopes, drift, inertia, and LFOs that make parameters behave like physical phenomena, with direct access to the note's expressive dynamics curve as a modulation source

The result is a synthesis environment where sounds are alive but controllable — where the act of building an instrument is itself a compositional choice, and the instrument responds to every note with the full expressiveness the composer put into the graph.
